description: 'phaelo' reports a bodily sensation or emotional state as someone experiences it. Pain and warmth can be felt, as can joy or unease. A 'mena ... meno' clause holds an impression as felt rather than asserted as fact. 'morae' (sense) instead reports what perception detects.
articulatory_notes: Bring the lips close without sealing them and pass unvoiced air between them for 'ph', then open into central 'a'. Give the following true-mid front 'e' its own stressed hiatus syllable. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l' and finish with rounded true-mid back 'o'.
usage_notes: A bodily sensation or emotion can stand directly before the verb. The complement frame 'mena ... meno phaelo' presents a complete proposition as an impression felt by the experiencer. In noun position, 'phaelo' is a feeling or felt experience.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Naming a sensation or emotion as something felt lets a speaker attend to it without turning it into a claim about the world.

semantic_domains.cognition: conscious awareness of an internal experience
semantic_domains.emotion: an emotional state as it is experienced
semantic_domains.physical: bodily sensation such as warmth or pain
